corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: Specifically, we train the generator to match the expected value of the features on an intermediate layer of the discriminator.

context_window.previous: Instead of directly maximizing the output of the discriminator, the new objective requires the generator to generate data that matches the statistics of the real data, where we use the discriminator only to specify the statistics that we think are worth matching.
context_window.current: Specifically, we train the generator to match the expected value of the features on an intermediate layer of the discriminator.
context_window.next: This is a natural choice of statistics for the generator to match, since by training the discriminator we ask it to find those features that are most discriminative of real data versus data generated by the current model.

top_candidates.sentence: The contributions made in this work are of a practical nature; we hope to develop a more rigorous theoretical understanding in future work.
The MNIST dataset contains 60, 000 labeled images of digits.
We present a variety of new architectural features and training procedures that we apply to the generative adversarial networks (GANs) framework.
We present a variety of new architectural features and training procedures that we apply to the generative adversarial networks (GANs) framework.
Using our new techniques, we achieve state-of-the-art results in semi-supervised classification on MNIST, CIFAR-10 and SVHN.

The generated images are of high quality as confirmed by a visual Turing test: our model generates MNIST samples that humans cannot distinguish from real data, and CIFAR-10 samples that yield a human error rate of 21.3%.
We also present ImageNet samples with unprecedented resolution and show that our methods enable the model to learn recognizable features of ImageNet classes.
In this work, we introduce several techniques intended to encourage convergence of the GANs game.
We hope that some of them may form the basis for future work, providing formal guarantees of convergence.
We propose several techniques to stabilize training that allow us to train models that were previously untrainable.
We apply our techniques to the problem of semi-supervised learning, achieving state-of-the-art results on a number of different data sets in computer vision.
Adding HA cannot prevent this problem.
